w:r.w:t: Powered by Blockchain, Smart Contracts & Client-Side Hash Verification
Client-side SHA-256 hashing — document never transmitted; unlimited entity types supported

The Decentralized KYC (Know Your Customer) System is a blockchain-based identity verification platform that enables a user to verify their identity once and reuse that verified identity across an unlimited number of institutions and entity types — without ever transmitting their original document to any party.

Version 3.0 expands the system from a two-node model (Node 1 + one additional bank) to a true Multi-Entity Ecosystem. The platform now natively supports any combination of entity types operating as verifying nodes, including:
Banks and financial institutions (retail, investment, neobanks)
Government portals (tax authorities, passport offices, e-government services)
DeFi and Web3 platforms (lending protocols, yield aggregators, DEXs)
Any other regulated or compliance-driven entity type

The system's core innovation remains its Privacy-Preserving Multi-Node Hash Verification Protocol. When a user uploads their identity document to the KYC DApp, a SHA-256 cryptographic hash is generated entirely within the user's browser. The document itself never leaves the user's device. Only this 64-character hash fingerprint is stored on the Ethereum blockchain.

When any entity node — regardless of type — needs to verify the same user, the user uploads their document to the DApp again. The DApp regenerates the hash client-side and sends only the hash to the entity's API endpoint. The entity node then cross-checks this hash against the immutable blockchain record, granting or denying access based on whether the hashes match. The original document is never seen by any entity node at any point.

A new on-chain Entity Registry allows the admin to register, configure, and manage any number of entity nodes across all supported entity types. Each entity is identified by type, has configurable permissions, and can be added or removed dynamically without redeploying the contract.

This design is fully GDPR-compliant, fraud-resistant, and production-grade — closely mirroring how enterprise identity systems like Sovrin and KILT Protocol operate in the real world, while extending their reach to the full breadth of the regulated economy.
Redundancy: Users submit identity documents to every bank, exchange, insurer, government portal, or platform separately
Document Exposure: Each new institution receives, stores, and processes a full copy of sensitive documents — multiplying breach risk with every new submission
No User Control: Users have no visibility or control over who holds copies of their passport, ID, or utility bills
High Cost: Financial institutions globally spend over $1.8 billion annually on KYC compliance
Slow Onboarding: Manual KYC can take 3–14 business days per institution
Centralized Data Silos: Each institution's KYC database is a honeypot for hackers
Sector Fragmentation: Banks, insurers, exchanges, and government agencies each run completely separate KYC processes — even for the same user
2.2 The Core Privacy Problem This System Solves

Even in systems that reuse KYC across institutions, the standard approach still requires the user to send their document to every subsequent institution. This means the document travels across networks and gets stored in multiple places — at every bank, every insurer, every exchange, every government portal.

This system solves that entirely and at scale. The document is hashed on the user's own device. No institution beyond Node 1 ever receives or stores the document — not the bank, not the crypto exchange, not the insurance company, not the government portal. Every entity node, regardless of type, only ever receives a 64-character mathematical fingerprint which is cryptographically useless without the original document.
2.3 The Multi-Entity Gap This Version Addresses

Version 2 demonstrated that a second node (a single extra bank) could verify a user via hash comparison. However, real-world identity reuse spans sectors, not just banks. A user who is KYC-verified at their primary bank should be able to instantly onboard to a crypto exchange, apply for insurance, access government services, and register on a DeFi protocol — all without ever submitting documents again.
Version 3 closes this gap by introducing a universal multi-entity architecture that treats all institutional types as first-class verifying nodes within a single unified system.
Client-side SHA-256 document hashing (document never leaves user's browser)
Smart contract for KYC registration, verification, hash storage, and revocation
On-chain Entity Registry supporting unlimited entity types: Banks, Crypto Exchanges, Insurance, Government, Healthcare, DeFi/Web3, Telecom, and Other
Multi-entity hash verification: any registered entity node can verify users using only hash comparison
User registration and document hash submission flow
Node 1 (Primary Verifier) approval and hash confirmation on-chain
Per-entity verification endpoint using blockchain hash cross-check
Entity-type-aware event emission (logs which entity type performed each check)
React.js frontend with MetaMask wallet integration and entity selector UI
Integration with real banking or government APIs
User-controlled selective disclosure per entity type
The multi-entity architecture introduces a wider set of stakeholders than the original two-node design. The following table captures all roles in the ecosystem:
Deploy contract, assign verifiers, manage entity registry
Bank, Govt body, or licensed KYC provider
Review off-chain documents, confirm document hash on-chain
One-time deep verification with hash locking
Upload doc to DApp, get hash generated, get verified by Node 1
Verify once — never send document again
Receive hash from DApp, cross-check blockchain, onboard customer
Instant compliant onboarding without storing documents
CEX or DEX with KYC requirements
Verify policyholder identity without re-collecting documents
Verify citizen KYC status for service delivery
Confirm patient identity for secure record access
Lightweight KYC gate for compliant DeFi participation
On-chain KYC without custody of PII
KYC for SIM registration or account opening
Audit on-chain verification history and events

When a user selects their identity document on the DApp, the frontend immediately computes a SHA-256 hash of the file using the browser's built-in Web Crypto API. The document itself is never uploaded to any server or transmitted anywhere — regardless of which entity type the user is interacting with.
Input: User selects file via file picker (passport, ID, utility bill, etc.)
Processing: SHA-256 hash computed entirely inside the browser using Web Crypto API
Output: 64-character hexadecimal hash string displayed to the user
Constraint: File never leaves the user's device at any point in this step
Performance: Hash generation completes in under 1 second for files up to 10MB
Any Ethereum wallet address can register by calling registerUser() with their name and the SHA-256 hash generated client-side. This creates a User record on-chain.
Output: UserRegistered event emitted on blockchain
Constraint: Each wallet address can only register once
FR-03: Node 1 Verification with Hash Confirmation

Node 1 (the primary verifier) approves the user after reviewing their document off-chain. During approval, Node 1 also submits the confirmed document hash — which must match what the user registered. This locks the verified hash permanently on-chain.
If match: isVerified = true, verifiedHash locked, verifiedBy = Node1 address
If mismatch: Transaction reverted with 'Hash mismatch — document not matching'
Output: UserVerified event emitted with verifier address and timestamp

This is the core innovation of v3. When any registered entity node — a bank, crypto exchange, insurance company, government portal, healthcare provider, DeFi platform, telecom operator, or any other type — needs to verify a user, the user uploads their document on the DApp. The DApp generates the hash client-side and sends only the hash to the entity's API endpoint. The entity calls checkDocumentHash() on the smart contract to cross-check.
Input: userAddress (address), submittedHash (string from DApp), entityType (enum)
Smart contract checks: submittedHash == verifiedHash AND isVerified == true AND entityType is registered
If all true: HashCheckPassed event emitted with entity type and address, returns true
If any false: HashCheckFailed event emitted with entity type and address, returns false
Critical: No entity node ever receives or stores the original document
Critical: The document hash is generated on the user's device — not on any entity's server
Critical: The same verifiedHash is used for all entity types — one hash, universal reuse
Any authorized verifier can revoke a user's KYC status at any time — for fraud, expired documents, or compliance violations. Revocation propagates instantly to all entity types.
Output: isVerified = false, UserRevoked event emitted permanently on-chain
Effect: All subsequent hash checks for this user return false immediately — across all entity types without exception
The admin manages the on-chain Entity Registry, which tracks all authorized verifying nodes across all entity types.
registerEntity(address, entityType, name, apiEndpoint): Admin registers a new entity node with its type classification
removeEntity(address): Admin removes an entity from the registry
updateEntityEndpoint(address, newEndpoint): Admin or entity updates API endpoint
getEntityInfo(address): Returns entity type, name, API endpoint, and status for any address
Entity types enum: BANK, CRYPTO_EXCHANGE, INSURANCE, GOVERNMENT, HEALTHCARE, DEFI, TELECOM, OTHER
Constraint: Only admin (contract deployer) can register or remove entities
addVerifier(address): Admin grants node verifier privileges (Node 1 capability)
Constraint: Only admin (contract deployer) can manage verifiers
MetaMask browser extension integration via ethers.js
Display connected wallet address and current network
Prompt user to switch to Sepolia testnet if on wrong network
Hash auto-generated on file selection — displayed to user for transparency
Clear label: 'Your document stays on your device — only the hash is submitted'
Submit calls registerUser(name, hash) via MetaMask transaction
Show transaction confirmation and Etherscan link
Entity selector: user chooses the entity type they want access from (Bank, Exchange, Insurance, Government, Healthcare, DeFi, Telecom, Other)
Entity instance selector: within each type, user picks the specific institution (e.g., HSBC, Binance, AXA, etc.)
User uploads their document — hash regenerated client-side
DApp sends only the hash + wallet address + entity type to the selected entity's API endpoint
DApp displays result: Access Granted or Access Denied with reason and entity-type-specific messaging
Document is never sent to any entity at any point
List of registered (pending) users with wallet addresses
One-click verify button — verifier submits confirmed hash from their off-chain review
Revoke button for any verified user — propagates to all entity types
Entity Registry management: add / remove entities, assign entity types, update API endpoints
On-chain event log: all verifications, revocations, hash checks — filterable by entity type
Enter any wallet address — see full KYC status
Shows: isVerified, verifiedAt timestamp, verifiedBy node address, document hash
Entity access log: shows which entity types have performed successful hash checks
Personal document transmitted to any entity node
Hash generation time (browser, up to 10MB file)
Smart contract read call response time
onlyRegisteredEntity modifier — unregistered entities cannot query
Unlimited — Banks, Exchanges, Insurers, Govt, Healthcare, DeFi, Telecom, etc.
Fully compliant — no PII on-chain or in transit
Time for any entity hash verification

The system follows a three-tier architecture with no traditional backend server. The smart contract is the backend — all business logic, entity registry, access control, and data storage happen on-chain. The only off-chain communication is when the DApp sends a hash (not a document) to an entity node's API endpoint for cross-checking.

In v3, the architecture scales horizontally across entity types. Each entity type (bank, exchange, insurer, etc.) operates its own independent API endpoint. All entity endpoints follow the same protocol: receive {walletAddress, documentHash}, call checkDocumentHash() on the smart contract, return the result. The smart contract is the single source of truth for all entity types simultaneously.
Core KYC logic, hash storage, entity registry, multi-entity verification
Client-side hash generation — document never leaves browser
User interface, entity selector, and hash generation UI
Per-entity API endpoints that receive hash, query blockchain, return result
Stores entity type, name, permissions, and API endpoint per verifier
struct User { string name; string documentHash; string verifiedHash; bool isVerified; uint256 verifiedAt; address verifiedBy; }
enum EntityType { BANK, CRYPTO_EXCHANGE, INSURANCE, GOVERNMENT, HEALTHCARE, DEFI, TELECOM, OTHER }
struct EntityInfo { EntityType entityType; string name; string apiEndpoint; bool isActive; }
mapping(address => User) public users — stores user record by wallet address
mapping(address => bool) public verifiers — tracks all authorized Node 1 verifiers
mapping(address => EntityInfo) public entityRegistry — stores entity info by entity address
mapping(address => EntityType[]) public userEntityAccess — tracks which entity types have accessed each user
documentHash: hash submitted by user at registration
verifiedHash: hash confirmed and locked by Node 1 at verification — this is what all entity nodes compare against
UserRegistered(address userAddress, string name) — fired on registration
UserVerified(address userAddress, address verifier) — fired when Node 1 verifies
HashCheckPassed(address userAddress, address checker, EntityType entityType) — fired when any entity node hash matches
HashCheckFailed(address userAddress, address checker, EntityType entityType) — fired when hash mismatch detected at any entity node
EntityRegistered(address entityAddress, EntityType entityType, string name) — fired when entity added to registry
EntityRemoved(address entityAddress, EntityType entityType) — fired when entity removed from registry
Step 1 — User connects MetaMask wallet to the KYC DApp
Step 2 — User enters their name and selects their identity document (passport, ID card, etc.)
Step 3 — DApp instantly computes SHA-256 hash of the document using Web Crypto API in the browser. Document never moves. Hash is displayed to the user for transparency.
Step 4 — User calls registerUser(name, hash) via MetaMask — transaction confirmed on blockchain
Step 5 — Node 1 (bank or KYC provider) reviews the actual document off-chain via email or their existing portal
Step 6 — Node 1 computes the same hash from the document they reviewed, then calls verifyUser(userAddress, confirmedHash) on the smart contract

Step 7 — Smart contract verifies: confirmedHash must match the user's registered hash. If match, isVerified = true and verifiedHash is locked permanently on-chain. If mismatch, transaction is reverted.
Step 8 — UserVerified event emitted. User is now KYC-verified on blockchain and eligible for verification by any registered entity node.
8.2 Multi-Entity Access Request Flow (Any Entity Type)
Step 1 — User wants access to an entity (e.g., a crypto exchange, an insurer, a government portal, a hospital, a DeFi protocol)
Step 2 — User visits the DApp and selects the entity type (e.g., CRYPTO_EXCHANGE) and the specific institution (e.g., Binance)
Step 3 — User uploads their identity document on the DApp again
Step 4 — DApp generates SHA-256 hash of the document client-side in the browser. Document is not sent anywhere.
Step 5 — DApp sends only the hash + user's wallet address + entity type to the selected entity's registered API endpoint
Step 6 — Entity's API calls checkDocumentHash(walletAddress, receivedHash, entityType) on the smart contract
Step 7 — Smart contract checks: does receivedHash match the verifiedHash on-chain? Is isVerified true? Is the calling entity registered?
Step 8 — If all true: HashCheckPassed event emitted with entity type and address; entity grants access. If any false: HashCheckFailed event emitted; entity denies access.
Step 9 — The entity never receives, stores, or processes the original document at any point.
Step 10 — The same flow works identically for Banks, Exchanges, Insurance companies, Government portals, Healthcare providers, DeFi platforms, Telecom operators, and any other registered entity type.

If a user attempts to submit a tampered or different document to any entity node, the SHA-256 hash generated will be different from the verified hash stored on blockchain. Even changing a single pixel of an image produces a completely different hash. The mismatch is detected instantly and a HashCheckFailed event — including the entity type — is permanently recorded on-chain. This mechanism is universal across all entity types.
Step 1 — A verifier detects fraud, expired documents, or a compliance violation
Step 2 — Verifier calls revokeUser(userAddress) on the smart contract
Step 3 — isVerified is set to false. UserRevoked event is permanently emitted on-chain.
Step 4 — All subsequent hash checks for this address return false immediately — across all entity types, all nodes, all sectors. Revocation is universal.
9.1 Privacy by Design — The Hash Protocol
Document hashing occurs exclusively client-side using the browser's native Web Crypto API
The original document is never transmitted to any server, entity node, or blockchain
All entity nodes — regardless of type — only ever receive a 64-character hex string
A SHA-256 hash cannot be reverse-engineered back into the original document
Fully compliant with GDPR Article 25 (Data Protection by Design and by Default)
The verifiedHash is locked on-chain by Node 1 at the time of verification
No one — not even the admin — can modify a stored hash after it is written
Any document alteration (even 1 pixel) produces a completely different SHA-256 hash
Hash mismatches are permanently recorded as HashCheckFailed events with entity type on-chain
The on-chain audit trail is immutable — fraud attempts cannot be erased and are visible to all entities
onlyAdmin modifier: Only contract deployer can add or remove verifiers and manage the entity registry
onlyVerifier modifier: Only whitelisted node addresses can verify, confirm hashes, or revoke
onlyRegisteredEntity modifier: Only addresses in the entity registry can call checkDocumentHash()
Hash match validation: verifyUser() reverts if Node 1's hash doesn't match user's registered hash
Entity type validation: checkDocumentHash() verifies the calling entity's type matches its registry entry
No Ether transfers in contract — eliminates reentrancy attack surface
Solidity ^0.8.0 — built-in overflow and underflow protection
MetaMask installed, Remix IDE ready, Sepolia testnet connected, test ETH obtained
KYC.sol written with multi-entity registry, entity type enum, per-entity hash verification, compiled and tested on Remix VM
Contract deployed to Sepolia, verified on Etherscan, all functions tested on testnet including multi-entity flows
React app with file picker, Web Crypto SHA-256 hashing, hash display, MetaMask connect, and entity selector UI
All flows working: register, Node 1 verify, multi-entity hash check (Bank/Exchange/Insurance/Govt/Healthcare), revoke, status display
Styled UI, error handling, hash mismatch fraud test across multiple entity types, edge case testing complete
README, architecture diagram, PRD v3, demo video, project report ready
Document transmitted to any entity node or blockchain
Smart contract deployed on Sepolia testnet
Node 1 can verify user and lock hash on-chain
Bank entity hash check against blockchain
Crypto exchange entity hash check against blockchain
Insurance entity hash check against blockchain
Government portal entity hash check against blockchain
Healthcare provider entity hash check against blockchain
DeFi / Web3 platform entity hash check against blockchain
Hash mismatch fraud detection working across all entity types
KYC revocation propagates to all entity nodes instantly
User uploads different file to any entity
Hash mismatch automatically detected and logged on-chain as HashCheckFailed for every entity
onlyRegisteredEntity modifier reverts call; entity must be whitelisted by admin first
Node 1 confirms wrong hash during verification
Smart contract rejects if confirmedHash != registeredHash — transaction reverts
Each entity operates its own independent API; failures are isolated and do not affect other entities
Browser Web Crypto API not supported
All modern browsers support Web Crypto API natively
Show install prompt; support multiple wallet providers via WalletConnect
Sepolia faucet unavailable for test ETH
Use multiple faucets: sepoliafaucet.com and Alchemy faucet as backup
Always copy fresh ABI from Remix after each redeploy; store ABI in config file
SHA-256 has 2^256 possible outputs — no practical collision has ever been found
NO — verify once, reuse across all entity types
NO — only 64-char hash for all entities
Banks, Exchanges, Insurance, Govt, Healthcare, DeFi, Telecom, and more
On-chain entity registry with per-type configuration
YES — user grants access to specific entity types
Universal hash mismatch detection across all entity types
Unified on-chain log across all entity types
Simple — no PII stored or transmitted anywhere
Seconds — same blockchain query for any entity type
Near zero — single hash comparison for all
Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKP): Prove identity attributes (e.g., 'I am over 18') without revealing the document or hash
IPFS Encrypted Storage: User optionally stores encrypted document on IPFS — hash of IPFS CID stored on-chain
W3C DID Standard: Replace wallet addresses with globally interoperable Decentralized Identifiers
Selective Disclosure: User chooses which attributes to share with each entity type (name only, age only, address only, etc.)
REST API Gateway: Allow traditional institutions across all sectors to query KYC status via standard HTTP
Multi-chain support: Deploy on Polygon or BNB Chain for near-zero gas fees
Mobile DApp: WalletConnect integration for mobile wallet users
Entity-type-specific permission scopes: e.g., Government can see full KYC; DeFi platforms only see verified/not-verified
Cross-entity analytics: Anonymized statistics on verification patterns across entity types
Integration with national ID systems: Aadhaar (India), EU Digital Identity Wallet
FATF Travel Rule compliance module for cross-border transactions
DAO governance for trusted entity onboarding across all entity types
SLA-backed uptime guarantees for enterprise entity API endpoints
Know Your Customer — mandatory identity verification process required by financial regulations
Decentralized, immutable ledger of transactions maintained by a peer-to-peer network
Self-executing code deployed on blockchain that runs automatically when conditions are met
Cryptographic hash function producing a unique 64-character fingerprint for any input file
Hashing performed in the user's browser — the original file never leaves the user's device
The first institution to perform full off-chain document verification and lock the hash on-chain
Any institution or platform (bank, exchange, insurer, government, healthcare, DeFi, telecom, etc.) that verifies a user using only hash comparison against blockchain
A classification of verifying institution (BANK, EXCHANGE, INSURANCE, GOVERNMENT, HEALTHCARE, DEFI, TELECOM, OTHER) registered in the entity registry on-chain
On-chain mapping that stores each registered entity's type, name, permissions, and API endpoint address
The document hash confirmed by Node 1 and permanently locked in the smart contract; this is what all entity nodes compare against
When the hash submitted by an entity node does not match the verifiedHash on blockchain — indicates tampered or wrong document
Browser-native cryptography API used to compute SHA-256 hashes without any external library
Browser extension wallet for interacting with Ethereum-based decentralized applications
Decentralized Identifier — a globally unique identifier owned and controlled by the user
High-level programming language for writing Ethereum smart contracts
A test version of Ethereum where fake ETH is used — no real money at risk
Transaction fee paid to Ethereum validators to execute a smart contract function
General Data Protection Regulation — EU law governing how personal data must be handled
Zero-Knowledge Proof — prove a fact without revealing the underlying data
Application Binary Interface — defines how a frontend communicates with a deployed smart contract
Anti-Money Laundering — regulatory requirement to detect and prevent financial crime, often combined with KYC
Decentralized KYC System | PRD v3.0 | Confidential | March 2025
